author: Jakob He
description: Agentic two-way sync for your vault and submodules with GitHub. AI resolves conflicts and git errors autonomously. - This plugin has not been manually reviewed by Obsidian staff.

manifest.author: Jakob
manifest.description: An LLM agent runs every git operation for your vault — commits, pulls, pushes, conflict resolution, recovery from broken repo states. Submodules supported. Conflict handling is one piece; the agent automates the whole sync pipeline.
manifest.name: Agentic Git Sync

name: Agentic Git Sync

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
## What's new in 1.3.22

### 🔗 GitHub App integration — install once, sync everywhere

Agentic Git Sync now has a dedicated GitHub App that handles authentication and repository access without personal tokens.

**[Install the GitHub App →](https://github.com/apps/agentic-git-sync)**

Once installed, click **Connect with GitHub App** in Settings → Account. The app grants fine-grained access to only the repos you choose, works across org accounts, and never expires.

---

### ✨ Zero-click PR creation (team mode)

The manual "Create PR" button is gone. Instead, every successful sync in team mode automatically opens a pull request on GitHub — no extra clicks required.

- Works for both manual syncs and the background auto-sync interval
- AI drafts the PR title and description from your commit history (falls back to last commit subject if no AI provider is configured)
- Already-open PRs are detected and skipped silently
- A brief notice with the PR link appears when a new PR is created

---

### 🎨 Redesigned GitHub App settings row

The Account section now shows two distinct states:

- **Disconnected** — "GitHub App · Recommended" badge with an "Install the app on GitHub →" link so new users know where to start
- **Connected** — a compact row per account with a ✓ check-circle, access list, and Refresh / Disconnect controls

---

### 🗂 PAT mode can now browse repositories

The repo picker (used in Add Submodule) now works with a Personal Access Token, not just the GitHub App. If you have a PAT configured, the browse button appears and lists all accessible repos. Selecting a repo also auto-fills the local folder name.

---

### Other fixes

- Settings page and setup wizard refresh automatically when a GitHub App connection completes — no manual reload needed
- Disconnecting the last GitHub App account no longer silently switches the auth method back to PAT

releases.author: leweii
releases.description:
Submodules can now stay current with a teammate's branch automatically, and individual submodules are now editable from Settings instead of requiring a `.github-sync.json` edit.

## Features

### Auto-merge from an upstream branch (`upstreamBranch`)
Each submodule can declare an optional **Upstream branch**. Every sync of that submodule will then `fetch origin/<upstreamBranch>` and merge it into the working branch before pushing. Built for the workflow where each teammate works on their own personal branch but wants to keep absorbing the team's `main` automatically.

- Set it in the **Add submodule** dialog (new field), or via the new **Edit** action on an existing submodule.
- Travels with the repo — recorded in `.github-sync.json`, so other machines pick it up on next clone.

### Manual \"Pull from…\" action per submodule
Each submodule card in the dashboard now has a pull-request icon button.
- Click it → modal lists the submodule's remote branches via the GitHub API (default branch first, working branch excluded).
- Pick a branch → plugin fetches it, merges into the submodule's current branch, pushes the result.

### Edit and delete submodules from Settings
**Settings → Submodules** now shows a pencil + trash button per row.
- **Pencil** opens an **Edit submodule** modal — change Remote URL / Branch / Upstream branch / Auto-sync. Saving rewrites `.gitmodules`, runs `git submodule sync`, updates the submodule's own `remote.origin.url`, seeds the new branch on the remote via the GitHub API if missing, then triggers a sync.
- **Trash** opens a confirm dialog that explains what will happen (deinit + working tree removal; the remote repo is not touched), then runs the existing remove flow.

### Conflicts route through the existing ConflictModal
Whether the conflict originates from a normal sync pull, an `upstreamBranch` auto-merge, or a manual \"Pull from…\" action, it lands in the same dashboard \"Resolve\" UI — including AI-assisted resolution when smart-sync is on.

## UX

- **Settings page redesigned around card-grouped sections.** Each section (\"Connected repository\", \"Submodules\", \"GitHub Account\", \"AI\", \"General\") now renders as a single rounded, bordered card with subtle 1px dividers between its items. Inter-section separation is just spacing — no horizontal rules. Matches the look of Obsidian's own community-plugin settings page.
- **Section titles** show a muted inline count where useful (e.g., `Submodules (2)`) instead of a separate description row.
- **PullFromBranchModal and EditSubmoduleModal** use the same header/body/footer pattern as the existing LocalChanges modal for visual consistency.

## Internal

- `GitManager.mergeFromBranch()` + `mergeAndPushFromBranch()` are the new merge primitives, shared by the `upstreamBranch` sync path and the manual Pull-from-branch action.
- `SubmoduleManager.setRemoteUrl()` repoints a submodule on both sides (parent `.gitmodules` and the submodule's own `remote.origin.url`) and invalidates the cached per-submodule GitManager.
- `Plugin.updateSubmodule()` applies metadata edits with the necessary git-level migration. `Plugin.surfaceConflict()` exposes the dashboard's Resolve flow to ad-hoc merge entry points that don't go through the scheduler.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
